---
title: "Conclusions"
---


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

The system works as designed on the question shape it was built for —
single-document, single-chunk citation against indexed regulatory and
corporate documents, with strict refusal when the corpus does not
answer the question. It does not yet work on cross-document
synthesis. Most of its remaining quality gap is upstream in
retrieval, not downstream in the language model. The work in this
report is as much a demonstration of engineering judgement under a
fixed budget as it is a demonstration of software: the retracted
claims, the deferred work, and the diagnostic discipline are the
deliverable as much as the code is.
:::

---

## What This Project Concludes {#sec-conclusions-headline}

A cited-RAG pipeline over six public regulatory and corporate
documents (15.3 MB; 461 chunks across four issuers) achieves, on a
70-question hand-crafted benchmark using open-weight local models on
Apple Silicon:

- **0.598 mean citation_recall** on the full answerable subset;
  **0.798** on the subset where retrieval succeeds upstream;
  **1.000** on single-chunk-retrievable questions.
- **104 of 104 refusal-category questions correctly refused** across
  the four-cell sweep, including the adjacent-but-unanswered and
  false-premise categories that are most prone to confabulation.
- **Zero hallucinated citations** on the production-default cell
  across the full 44-question answerable sweep.
- **No pipeline stage requiring a remote call**, including the
  evaluation.

These numbers are not, in absolute terms, headline-grabbing — the
system has a real ~60% top-line — but they are precise about *where*
the system works and *where* it does not. The 0.798 answer-stage
ceiling is the more honest read of what the language model can do
when given correctly-retrieved chunks; the 0.598 headline reflects
retrieval failures upstream that no answer-model swap can fix.

The work demonstrates that **cited RAG on regulatory and corporate
disclosure documents is tractable with open-weight local models**,
and that the dominant first-order constraint is retrieval, not
generation.

---

## What This Work Changes {#sec-conclusions-changes}

For a reinsurer evaluating cited-RAG approaches for underwriting
research, three concrete things change:

- **The citation contract can be structurally enforced.** Citation
  validation by set-membership against the retrieved context is
  cheap, deterministic, and catches confabulation regardless of
  whether the language model honoured the prompt instruction. A
  deployment does not have to trust the model to cite correctly; it
  can structurally verify.

- **The refusal contract can be structurally enforced too.** The
  exact-phrase detector is brittle by design and works precisely
  because of that brittleness. Both candidate models honour the
  contract in 100% of refusal benchmark cases across both prompt
  versions. The refusal partition into three adversarial categories
  (out-of-corpus, adjacent-but-unanswered, false-premise) is the
  load-bearing part — the contract is tested under attack, not just
  under cooperative conditions.

- **Local-first execution is a real architectural property.** All
  inference — retrieval, embedding, generation — runs on the
  operator's hardware after first weight pull. For a deployment that
  cannot send underwriting queries to a third-party API, this is a
  hard requirement, and the v1 system shows the requirement does not
  prevent a working artefact.

What the work does *not* change is the cross-document synthesis
problem, which remains hard for every model tested at every prompt
version. A reinsurer relying on cross-document synthesis as a
primary use case should not be using this system today.

---

## On the Methodology {#sec-conclusions-methodology}

Two claims this project once held have been retracted on the record
(Sections 5 and 8). A third claim — that the system's headline
number is dominated by the answer model — was diagnosed as wrong
during Q12 and replaced with the correct diagnosis (retrieval).
The discipline that produced these corrections is, in some ways,
the most transferable thing in the report.

The discipline has three moving parts:

- **Pre-stated falsification criteria.** A claim that cannot say
  in advance what evidence would force its retraction is not a
  claim; it is a position. D014 specified the falsification
  criterion before the sweep ran; the criterion was published in
  `decisions.md`; the criterion fired; the claim was retracted.
- **Append-only history with timestamp and commit hash.** The
  journal entry that records each retraction is appended, not edited
  back over the original claim. A reviewer doing a "what did this
  project get wrong?" audit can read the original claim and the
  retraction side by side. This is uncomfortable; it is also the
  only way to make the audit possible.
- **Honest framing of partial passes.** The N=40 hallucination arm
  of the D014 criterion technically failed (because 2 × 0 = 0), but
  the spirit of the criterion was met (15 → 3 is a 5× reduction).
  Section 5 records both the technical fail and the qualitative
  pass openly. A tidied conclusion would have been shorter; the
  honest one is more useful.

This discipline is the deliverable
the report is meant to demonstrate. It works the same way under
positive evidence as under negative evidence — the same machinery
that catches the family-axis retraction would catch a future v2
claim. It is, in effect, the project's epistemic infrastructure.

---

## What Comes Next {#sec-conclusions-next}

The v2 work-stream prioritises the largest source of headline
weakness. From Section 8, in order:

1. **Close the retrieval ceiling (Q13).** Three remediation paths
   identified; cheapest first (HyDE-style query expansion, ~1–2
   hours). The eleven retrieval-miss questions on the N=70 benchmark
   are the test set; the order-of-magnitude question for v2 is "how
   many of those eleven the remediation surfaces in top-5."
2. **LLM-as-judge semantic scoring (Q10.3).** Adds the dimension
   structural scoring cannot reach. Right sequencing: run it on the
   existing canonical N=70 runs first, see how much it shifts the
   headline, then decide on integration.
3. **Multi-seed variance estimates.** Half a day of eval rerunning
   plus aggregation code; produces calibrated precision claims.
4. **Index the synthetic Sycamore documents and extend the
   benchmark accordingly.**
5. **Corpus and benchmark expansion** with more issuers and more
   questions across more authors.
6. **Operational hardening** for any move toward multi-user
   deployment.

The lower-priority items — GEPA/DSPy systematic prompt optimisation
(Q10), the BGE-M3 multi-vector revisit (Q7) — remain open but no
longer load-bearing after D014's hand-designed v2 prompt closed
most of the gap they were originally posed against.

---

## A Note on What This Artefact Is For {#sec-conclusions-artefact}

This report describes a proof of concept built for a Lead
initial scope reinsurance underwriting copilot prototype. The budget
is not an apology; it is the constraint that shapes everything in
the report. Within initial scope a single engineer cannot ship a
production-ready underwriting copilot. What can be shipped is
evidence of how the engineer makes decisions under budget — what
gets built, what gets rejected, what gets deferred, what gets
retracted, and on what evidence.

The documentation set (`charter.md`, `decisions.md`,
`architecture.md`, `governance.md`, `security.md`, `evaluation.md`,
`journal.md`, `status.md`, `philosophy.md`, `open_questions.md`,
`backlog.md`) is unusually heavy relative to the codebase, and
this is deliberate. It is the non-code half of the evidence. A
reader who finds the documentation excessive is reading the
artefact as if it were code-only; a reader who finds it
load-bearing is reading it the way it was intended.

If the report leaves a reader with the right picture of what was
built, why it was built that way, what works, what does not, and
what would have to be true for the conclusions to change, the
artefact has done its job. None of this requires the system itself
to be ready for production — only for the engineering judgement
behind it to be visible.
